text: Exercise training robustly increases VO2max at all intensities, including both low intensity and high intensity protocols.
High intensity training produces small to moderate beneficial effects on VO2max compared to low intensity training.

The apparent VO2max advantage of high intensity over low intensity training is moderated by training variables beyond intensity itself, including training impulse, interval length, training volume, and program duration.
Participant baseline characteristics such as age and fitness level moderate the relative effectiveness of high intensity versus low intensity training on VO2max improvement.
When confounding training variables are accounted for, differences between low intensity and high intensity protocols on VO2max are either small, trivial, or inconclusive.
Evidence from 179 primary studies across 11 systematic reviews confirms that training intensity alone is not the primary determinant of VO2max improvement; total training stimulus matters.
